title: Can we predict success from log data in VLEs? Classification of interactions for learning analytics and their relation with performance in VLE-supported F2F and online learning

text.content: Can we predict success from log data in VLEs? Classification of interactions for learning analytics and their relation with performance in VLE-supported F2F and online learning

title.plain_text: Can we predict success from log data in VLEs? Classification of interactions for learning analytics and their relation with performance in VLE-supported F2F and online learning

tables.heading: Limitations
rows: [Student–student, Student–content, Student–teacher, Student-system, Transmission of content, Creating class interactions,  Evaluating students, Evaluation of teachers and courses, Computer-based instruction, Active, Passive]

Categorization of the logs. Logs were categorized in 3 perspectives:

Agent; Frequency; Mode
Not clustering, but authors distinguish between F2F courses and Online Courses.

Performance in F2F courses is not predicted by any of the variables extracted from the LMS.

On Online Courses, all variables related to Agent, Mode and Frequency were found to be significant at alpha=0.05, with Transmission of content being the only variable not significant at 0.01.

It is necessary to study moderating effects of interactions in Online courses and additional data sources are needed to predict performance in F2F settings.

Identifying the most effective types of interactioins with the system may help course creators to re-design their courses to correct undesired behaviors.

There is potential is proceeding with better eploration of variables that explain performance, but also create recommender systems that benefit students.

Features extracted refer to types of interactions. 

No relationship could be found between any of the features and student final score in Face-to-Face Contexts.

Authors make a comparison between different forms of Log classification and try to find what are ones that best predict performance.

Authors argue that promoting the types of interactions that correlate with performance would be helpful.

Approach only reveals to be effective for online contexts, not VLE-Supported F2F.

Validation in other educational settings. 

Limited data sources.

Interactions are only evaluated by their type, with no specifics about the content of the interaction.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: A big challenge in learning analytics is understanding whether an interaction is meaningful for learning or not. Authors focus on understanding which interactions are meaningful and which aren’t. For that, they develop a nomenclature distinguishing between Online Learning contexts and F2F supported by VLE contexts.

text.content: A big challenge in learning analytics is understanding whether an interaction is meaningful for learning or not. Authors focus on understanding which interactions are meaningful and which aren’t. For that, they develop a nomenclature distinguishing between Online Learning contexts and F2F supported by VLE contexts.

Authors focus on understanding which interactions are meaningful and which aren’t. For that, they develop a nomenclature distinguishing between Online Learning contexts and F2F supported by VLE contexts.

blocks.text: Authors found that, indeed, there are pedagogically meaningful interactions in Online Learning but failed to obtain similar results for F2F supported by VLE contexts.

blocks.text: It is difficult to identify what are the contributions of different types of interaction to student learning. In traditional learning paradigms, the teacher is often the guide by which learning occurs as the professor is the mediator between student and content.

blocks.text: The authors argue that the latter 2 are more easily enhanced in e-learning scenarios. Moreover, these types of interactions on face-to-face scenarios are often informal which makes it really difficult to quantify their impact towards learning. LMS sort of facilitate this as they record interactions.

blocks.text: The authors view LA as a field focused on the study on VLE logs. It emerged from the adoption of VLE in educational institutions and the proliferation of DM techniques. Since the goal of learning analytics is, at the end of the day, to understand and improve learning processes it is essential to understand and contextualize the types of interactions that exist between student and the VLE. The authors identify main types of approaches in LA:

blocks.text: Over time, researchers have acknowledged that the interactions of learners with LMS or VLEs have some form of pedagogical meaning. However, researchers do not agree on what types of interactions have pedagogical meaning, what are the criteria for selection of relevant types of interactions. Thus, the proposed RQs are :

blocks.text: Methods

text.content: Methods

rich_text.plain_text: Methods

blocks.text: The first part of the paper stems from validating the 3 previously identified frameworks for classification of interactions and see whether these map to differences in student performance. The authors use data from 6 online courses (138 students) and 2 face-to-face courses (218 students).

text.content: The first part of the paper stems from validating the 3 previously identified frameworks for classification of interactions and see whether these map to differences in student performance. The authors use data from 6 online courses (138 students) and 2 face-to-face courses (218 students).

rich_text.plain_text: The first part of the paper stems from validating the 3 previously identified frameworks for classification of interactions and see whether these map to differences in student performance. The authors use data from 6 online courses (138 students) and 2 face-to-face courses (218 students).

blocks.text: Authors use Moodle for all the courses. Traditionally, the materials gradually become available as the semester advances but remain available afterward.

blocks.text: Data Extraction

text.content: Data Extraction

rich_text.plain_text: Data Extraction

blocks.text: Moodle records the logs of interactions between students and content. The authors resorted to extracting the logs. Filtering out of interactions according to the designated taxonomies is, at this stage not possible and requires additional pre-processing.

text.content: Moodle records the logs of interactions between students and content. The authors resorted to extracting the logs. Filtering out of interactions according to the designated taxonomies is, at this stage not possible and requires additional pre-processing.

rich_text.plain_text: Moodle records the logs of interactions between students and content. The authors resorted to extracting the logs. Filtering out of interactions according to the designated taxonomies is, at this stage not possible and requires additional pre-processing.

blocks.text: Each possible interaction has been manually labelled by 2 experts. These classifications were subsequently assessed by 3 additional domain experts. The resulting categorizations are integrated in a Moodle Plugin in order to return the classified interactions. In essence, the work starts with manually labelling all of the interactions.

blocks.text: The authors look at the categorizations under the 3 perspectives. Most of the interactions in F2F contexts have very little correlation with the final grade and, in fact, none have statistically significant results.

text.content: The authors look at the categorizations under the 3 perspectives. Most of the interactions in F2F contexts have very little correlation with the final grade and, in fact, none have statistically significant results.

rich_text.plain_text: The authors look at the categorizations under the 3 perspectives. Most of the interactions in F2F contexts have very little correlation with the final grade and, in fact, none have statistically significant results.

blocks.text: However, for an online context, the types of interactions do seem to be linearly correlated with the final grade, with all correlations being moderate.

text.content: However, for an online context, the types of interactions do seem to be linearly correlated with the final grade, with all correlations being moderate.

rich_text.plain_text: However, for an online context, the types of interactions do seem to be linearly correlated with the final grade, with all correlations being moderate.

blocks.text: When it comes to the regression, no predictive power was found in any of the types of interactions. In essence, the authors attempted 3 forms of categorization of the logs and attempted to see which led to the best predictions of the final grade via a Multiple Linear Regression.

blocks.text: Discussion

text.content: Discussion

rich_text.plain_text: Discussion

blocks.text: The authors' goal was to establish a course-independent nomenclature for labeling VLE interactions, which could indicate the importance of specific interactions. Despite the difficulty, they consider that they have been successful.

text.content: The authors' goal was to establish a course-independent nomenclature for labeling VLE interactions, which could indicate the importance of specific interactions. Despite the difficulty, they consider that they have been successful.

rich_text.plain_text: The authors' goal was to establish a course-independent nomenclature for labeling VLE interactions, which could indicate the importance of specific interactions. Despite the difficulty, they consider that they have been successful.

blocks.text: This comes from the fact that they identify 3 types of interaction types that exhibit predictive potential of a student’s final grade. In more detail, they even find that Agent interaction type classification to be an effective way of labelling interactions of students for ONLINE contexts.
